data.title: Tillis continues to hold Fed nominees despite latest offer from Treasury
data.description:
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.), a senior member of the Senate Banking Committee, says he will keep holding up President Trump’s nominees to the Federal Reserve until the criminal investigation of Fed Chair Jerome Powell is resolved.

This poses a significant obstacle to Trump’s plan to appoint Kevin Warsh as the next chair of the nation’s central bank to succeed Powell when his term is expires in May.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent this week floated a proposal to shift the Powell probe from the Justice Department (DOJ) to the Banking Committee, a proposal that Republicans described as a “trial balloon” to keep Warsh’s nomination on track for Senate confirmation this spring.

Tillis this week welcomed the offer to give the Banking Committee more oversight of the Fed renovations, but he clarified that he will not drop his hold on Warsh or any Fed nominee while federal prosecutors are investigating the Fed chief for cost overruns.

“I don’t think it’s an offramp, I think it’s a suggestion for good governance. I don’t think we do enough oversight on how our plans work out in terms of cost of time,” Tillis said of Bessent’s offer. “I think an oversight hearing on this would be fine.”

But the North Carolina Republican rejected the idea that he would drop his blockade of Fed nominees while Powell faces potential criminal charges from federal prosecutors over renovations at the Fed headquarters.

“It doesn’t change my posture,” he said, arguing that Trump should be “angry” that U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia Jeanine Pirro issued grand jury subpoenas to the Fed “who didn’t at least go up through the chain at DOJ.”

“I believe that somebody probably was motivated by wanting to be the MVP for undermining the independence of the Fed and they thought this would be a way to do it,” he continued. “Well, that’s just another example of an amateur who doesn’t understand the second- and third-order effects.”

Tillis emphasized that the Treasury Department’s trial balloon won’t be enough to get him to drop his holds.

“I made it very clear that the only way we move forward on any Fed nom … [is] until this is resolved,” he said of the criminal investigation. “If we undermine the independence of the Fed, there will be catastrophic economic consequences.”

Tillis announced last month that he would block all of Trump’s nominees to the Fed until the DOJ concluded its criminal probe of Powell over his management of the Fed’s headquarters in Washington.

The senator warned the investigation threatened to undermine the independence of the bank and the credibility of DOJ.

He called it out on the Senate floor as a “vindictive prosecution” and “wrong.”

Bessent during a Friday interview with CNBC’s “Squawk Box” called on the Banking Committee to proceed with a hearing on Warsh’s nomination, even though Sen. Tim Scott (R-S.C.), who chairs the committee, doesn’t have the votes he needs to advance Warsh’s nomination without Tillis’s support — assuming Democrats also vote no on moving the nominee.

The Treasury secretary said he expects that Republicans on the Banking panel “are going to proceed” with the hearing and revealed there’s an agreement to begin processing the nomination.

“I think it’s important to get the hearings underway, and I think we have an agreement to do that,” he added.

data.title: Trump pardons 5 former NFL players
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday issued pardons for five former NFL players.

The former players include Joe Klecko, Nate Newton, Jamal Lewis, Travis Henry and the late Billy Cannon. President Trump’s pardon czar, Alice Marie Johnson, announced the news on social platform X. 

“As football reminds us, excellence is built on grit, grace, and the courage to rise again. So is our nation,” Johnson wrote. “So is our nation.”

She added that Dallas Cowboys general manager Jerry Jones “personally” broke the news to Newton, a former offensive lineman for the team. The 64-year-old served 30 months in prison on a federal drug trafficking charge starting in 2002. 

Since his release, Newton has been active at the Cowboys’ events and participated in media appearances for the team, according to the Fort Worth Star-Telegram. 

Cannon, a former football star who died in 2018, served two-and-a-half years in prison after he admitted to participating in a counterfeiting scheme in 1983. He played for the Houston Oilers, Oakland Raiders and Kansas City Chiefs and was a two-time All-Pro and two-time Pro-Bowler, according to ESPN.

Klecko is a former defensive lineman for the New York Jets who was sentenced to three months in prison for committing perjury in an insurance fraud case. He was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 2023. 

Lewis, who played for the Baltimore Ravens and the Cleveland Browns, was charged in 2004 for attempting to set up a drug deal. The former running back won the Super Bowl with the Ravens in 2001. 

Henry, a former running back for the Buffalo Bills, pled guilty in 2009 for conspiracy to traffic cocaine and was sentenced to three years in prison for financially backing a drug ring. 

“Grateful to @POTUS for his continued commitment to second chances,” Johnson said later in the post. “Mercy changes lives.”

Johnson was issued clemency by Trump herself after she was sentenced to life for a nonviolent drug offense. After a petition from reality TV star Kim Kardashian, the president commuted her sentence during his first term. In February, he named Johnson as pardon czar.

During his first stretch in the White House, Trump approved 238 clemency requests — far less than former President Obama, who granted 1,927, according to Pew Research. Former President Biden granted more clemency requests than any prior president, with a total of 4,245. 

Trump has made several controversial pardons during his second term, including granting around 1,500 requests from those tied to the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the Capitol.

data.title: Trump administration lowering Taiwan tariffs in new trade deal
data.description:
The U.S. has reached a reciprocal trade agreement with Taiwan, with Taiwan agreeing to eliminate or reduce nearly all its tariff barriers on U.S. goods, according to the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR).

The U.S. agreed to lower its tariff rate on most goods from Taiwan to 15 percent in exchange for Taiwan eliminating or reducing 99 percent of tariff barriers and providing preferential market access for some U.S. industrial and agricultural exports.

Taiwan also committed to increasing its purchases of U.S. goods through 2029, including $44.4 billion in liquefied natural gas and crude oil, $15.2 billion in civil aircraft and engines, and $25.2 billion in power equipment, power grids, and other equipment, according to a USTR fact sheet.

“President Trump’s leadership in the Asia-Pacific region continues to generate prosperous trade ties for the United States with important partners across Asia, while further advancing the economic and national security interests of the American people,” U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a statement.

He hailed the agreement as providing more opportunities for American farmers, ranchers, fishermen, workers, small business owners and manufacturers.

“This Agreement also builds on our longstanding economic and trade relationship with Taiwan and will significantly enhance the resilience of our supply chains, particularly in high-technology sectors,” Greer added. “I want to thank my counterparts from Taiwan for their strong commitment to achieving fair and balanced trade with the United States.”

Greer attended Thursday’s signing alongside Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, as well as Taiwan’s Vice Premier Li-chiun Cheng and Minister without Portfolio Jen-ni Yang.

The announcement comes weeks after the Commerce Department said a separate trade deal had been reached with Taiwan, in which the country committed to investing billions of dollars in U.S. chip manufacturing.

Under that deal, Taiwanese companies are set to invest at least $250 billion in building out advanced semiconductor, energy and AI capacity in the U.S., and the Taiwanese government will also provide at least $250 billion in credit guarantees for additional investment.

Lutnick said at the time that the goal was for the U.S. to become “self-sufficient in the capacity of building semiconductors.”

data.title: The midterms aren't enough — Democrats must campaign for the White House
data.description:
George Washington’s tenacity in winning our war of independence (with French help), after losing many battles, forms the dramatic arc of Ken Burn’s gripping documentary, “The American Revolution.”

Looking ahead to this year’s midterm elections, Democrats should take the long view like Washington. As important as it is to win the House and possibly the Senate in November, it’s even more crucial for Democrats to take back the White House in 2028.  

Taking control of the House would give heretofore impotent Democrats some ability to check President Trump’s flagrant abuses of presidential power. They could freeze funding for his outlandish decrees and probe his brazen politicization of federal law enforcement agencies and meddling in state elections.

The party out of power usually makes gains in midterm elections, and Democrats need only flip three seats to control the House. They are also riding a tailwind from Trump’s unpopular policies. By large margins, the public disapproves of his handling the economy, inflation and his signature issue, immigration. 

Yet Trump’s fall doesn’t signal Democrats’ rise. Voters still trust Republicans more to address most of their top concerns. That’s why even a House and Senate sweep wouldn’t stop today’s realignment of U.S. politics along educational lines. It’s given Republicans a structural advantage because their base — non-college voters — constitute a supermajority (nearly 60 percent) and are spread more evenly across the country.

The strategic imperative for Democrats now is building a large and lasting majority that can defeat MAGA populism decisively. Otherwise, power will keep bouncing back and forth between two minority parties responsive only to their most fervent partisans.

Democratic strategist Doug Sosnik notes that either the House, Senate or White House has changed hands in 11 of the last 13 elections. To break this impasse, Democrats need a singular focus on winning back non-college voters. But the party’s establishment, unwilling to incur progressive wrath, seems content with managing a shrinking coalition. 

Emblematic of such complacency is the Democratic National Committee’s decision not to release its 2024 election post-mortem. This is a disservice to the party. Democrats need to face facts: by moving left on economics, on culture and on climate, the Biden-Harris administration alienated working Americans, including a distressing number of Latino and Black voters.  

They also should get out of Washington and learn from rising party leaders who are winning working class voters and governing successfully. Last week, my organization took its “New Directions” party renewal conversation to Michigan, where pragmatic Democrats hold most of the top offices in a blue-collar state that Trump won twice. In 2000, Michigan ranked 16th in the nation in terms of per capita income. Now it has fallen to 40th place.  

Trump claims that his tariffs are bringing back yesterday’s factory jobs, but Michiganders know better. As my colleague Ed Gresser noted, manufacturing employment in Michigan and nationally fell last year, as Trump’s tariffs raised the costs of making things in America.  

Democrats owe working people who have suffered downward mobility more than performative attacks on billionaires and new handouts from Washington. What we heard in Michigan is that Democrats need a better economic deal focused on helping working Americans make ends meet and get ahead, participate more fully in private sector growth and use innovations like AI to boost their productivity and pay.  

On immigration, Democrats are right to demand an end to brutal enforcement tactics. But where do they stand on the underlying issue of securing the border and enforcing U.S. immigration laws? Democrats must reassure voters they won’t repeat the Biden administration’s colossal error of making it easy for people to enter the country without permission. They should reaffirm the Obama and Clinton administration’s resolute opposition to illegal immigration. And they should reject “Sanctuary City” declarations as a gratuitous exercise in progressive moral preening.

On crime and public order, Democrats likewise need to distance themselves from the left’s anti-police rhetoric, vow swift and certain punishment for lawbreakers and dismantle homeless encampments that disfigure our cities.

Democrats also should affirm that our shared national identity as Americans is more important than our different race, ethnic and gender identities. And they should oppose attempts to excommunicate Democrats who express common sense qualms about transgender operations for children.

The party should also acknowledge that climate alarmism has failed to convince working class voters to embrace policies they see as producing energy scarcity and high fuel bills. Democrats instead should use the full suite of fuels and clean technologies to meet rising energy demand (including for data centers) while reducing carbon emissions on more realistic timetables.

However satisfying, a midterm win is not enough. Like Washington, Democrats should keep their eyes on the grand prize: electing a pragmatic Democratic president who can reunite the country and resume its forward progress.

Will Marshall is founder and president of Progressive Policy Institute.

data.title: Live updates: DHS gears up for shutdown; Trump to visit troops at Fort Bragg
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is gearing up for a shutdown after Senate Democrats on Thursday blocked bills that would keep the lights on.

Senators left Washington without a deal, showing just how far apart Democrats and White House negotiators are on a potential agreement to reform Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) after two fatal shootings in Minneapolis heightened scrutiny of DHS.

Starting at midnight Saturday, key agencies overseen by the department — including the Transportation Security Administration, Federal Emergency Management Agency, U.S. Coast Guard and U.S. Secret Service, among others — will see a lapse in funding.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) told their colleagues to be prepared to return to the Capitol on short notice if an agreement is reached.

President Trump on Friday will visit Fort Bragg in North Carolina and speak to military families and the specialized troops that assisted with the ouster of embattled Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro earlier this year.

Trump will then travel to his Mar-a-Lago resort in Palm Beach, Fla., for the weekend.

Follow along live below.

data.title: Where are all the 'Don't tread on me' Americans?
data.description:
There’s a reason for what we are seeing in Minneapolis and elsewhere in the U.S. It’s the result of the Trump administration’s belief that illegal immigrants don’t have the same constitutional rights as the rest of us.

As President Trump himself put it, “If people come into our country illegally there’s a different standard.” The general counsel of the Department of Homeland Security even claims there is “broad judicial recognition that illegal aliens aren’t entitled to the same Fourth Amendment protections as U.S. citizens.”

That’s factually incorrect. There is no such broad judicial recognition. But it also completely misunderstands the point of having constitutional rights in the first place. 

If you could somehow magically determine, with perfect accuracy, who was guilty of a crime and where they had hidden the evidence, you wouldn’t need the Fourth Amendment, which protects people against unreasonable searches and seizures. Nor would you need due process, which protects people against being arbitrarily punished by the government. 

But we lack such magical powers, and so we have a package of constitutional rights that we created to keep the government from becoming oppressive.

These rights aren’t just a defense against an intentionally tyrannical government; they also protect us against well-meaning bureaucrats. As Judge Alex Kozinski put it, “Liberty — the freedom from unwarranted intrusion by government — is as easily lost through insistent nibbles by government officials who seek to do their jobs too well as by those whose purpose it is to oppress; the piranha can be as deadly as the shark.”

So is getting immigrant criminals off the street a justification for ICE’s behavior? Constitution says no. Of course, some immigrants are criminals who shouldn’t be on the streets. Some Americans are criminals who shouldn’t be on the streets either. Nonetheless, we have a Constitution that prevents police from roving those streets and demanding that people present their papers, or from breaking into someone’s house without probable cause and a warrant signed by a judge.

Those rights don’t exist to protect criminals. They exist to protect innocent people. And it is innocent people who suffer when those rights are ignored, whether the government is hunting criminals or immigrants.

Just ask the innocent, elderly American citizen dragged into the snow, half naked, after ICE broke into his house.

Let me put it another way. Over 250 years, we have carefully evolved rules for law enforcement that protect our constitutional rights and protect ordinary Americans from abuse by the government that is supposed to protect them. In 2023, there were 22,830 murders committed in the U.S. The best estimate is that about 250 of those were committed by illegal immigrants.

It’s obvious that garden-variety murderers are a far greater threat to Americans’ safety — almost a hundred times greater — than illegal alien murderers are. But we would never agree to discarding the Fourth Amendment and due process to enforce regular criminal law. The whole idea is offensive.

So why is the Trump administration demanding that we abandon constitutional rights to go after people in the country illegally?

I’m not upset because Trump is deporting criminals. When I look at what’s going on in Minneapolis and elsewhere, I see law-abiding American citizens being tear-gassed and beaten. I see masked men roaming the streets and demanding to see people’s papers. I see people gunned down for no good reason at all. 

I see people who have every right to be here swept up and detained. I see ICE and the federal government wilfully ignoring court orders meant to prevent injustices in individual cases. 

Alex Pretti and Renee Good are just the tip of the iceberg. While American citizen Miramar Martinez wasn’t killed, new evidence released on Tuesday shows that her shooting in Chicago by federal agents last October was even more shocking in that it might have been planned in advance.

All this isn’t just a threat to illegal immigrants. It is a threat to all of us.

I have a challenge to all the patriots out there who fly “Don’t Tread on Me” flags and talk about upholding the Constitution. This is your moment. You’ve spent your entire lives talking about resisting tyranny. Now it is happening right before your eyes.

Pretti was not just a cosplay patriot. He dared to exercise the First and Second Amendment rights you claim to believe in. And they killed him for it.

What are you going to do about it? 

All this can still be stopped before it is too late. And you are the ones to stop it, because Trump will listen to you in a way that he won’t listen to anyone else. 

If you oppose tyranny, the people of Minneapolis are your brothers and sisters in arms, regardless of what you think about immigration. They are standing up to a government that has gone too far. They are putting their lives on the line to do it. Are you going to stand with them, or was it all just talk?

Chris Truax is an appellate attorney who served as Southern California chair for John McCain’s primary campaign in 2008.

data.title: Homan on ICE Minneapolis pullback: 'If we need to come back, we'll come back'
data.description:
Border czar Tom Homan signaled Thursday that the decision to end President Trump’s surge of immigration enforcement officers to Minnesota does not mean they will be leaving the state entirely.

Homan told Fox News host Laura Ingraham that “hundreds of special agents” will continue the Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) investigation into fraud allegations tied to the state’s social services programs.

The administration will also continue to investigate a protest at a St. Paul, Minn., church last month that led to multiple arrests, including those of independent journalists Don Lemon and Georgia Fort.

“This is ending the surge, but we’re not going away,” Homan said Thursday on the “Ingraham Angle.”

“And let me say this, over 800 flights a day land in St Paul, Minnesota; if we need to come back, we’ll come back,” he added.

The border czar announced the day before that Operation Metro Surge was set to conclude following weeks of growing unrest sparked by the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis by federal agents.  

Tensions over the tactics being used in immigration enforcement operations spilled onto Capitol Hill, where Democrats have stonewalled bills to fund DHS over demands for new restrictions on Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers.

All but one Democrat, Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), voted against advancing a full-year DHS spending bill on Thursday, a day before the Feb. 13 deadline, paving the way for a likely shutdown of the department.

“They care more about politics than the safety and security of this nation,” Homan said of Democrats.

A DHS shutdown would have few implications for immigration agencies, as ICE already secured $75 billion in the One Big, Beautiful Bill Act signed by Trump last summer to continue operating through 2029. 

Instead, other federal agencies within the department could be brought to a halt, including the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), the U.S. Coast Guard and the U.S. Secret Service, among others.

“ICE has all the money they want to keep doing the job,” Homan said Thursday. “Of course, the men and women of Border Patrol and ICE won’t be getting paid but will continue to do operations.”

“And let me be clear, mass deportations will continue, and we’re not rolling back,” he continued. “President Trump promised mass deportations, and that’s exactly what the American people are going to get.”

data.title: Annual inflation rate fell to 2.4 percent in January, below expectations
data.description:
Consumer prices rose 2.4 percent over the past year and 0.2 percent in January alone, according to data released Friday by the Labor Department.

The latest consumer price index (CPI) report, which was delayed by the brief government shutdown earlier this month, showed inflation coming in below expectations.

Economists expected the annual inflation rate to fall to 2.5 percent in January from 2.7 percent in December, and monthly inflation to hold steady at 0.3 percent, according to consensus estimates.

The new inflation figures come as President Trump and Republicans face pressure to show progress against rising prices. 

The annual inflation rate has lingered close to 2.6 percent since November 2024, when Trump rode backlash against record high price growth to another term in the White House.

A little more than a year later, Trump has faced his own reckoning on inflation as his economic approval ratings drop and Democratic candidates post major victories in off-year elections.

A survey from NPR/PBS News/Marist released last week found that 59 percent of respondents disapprove of the president’s performance on the economy, a record high for disapproval and an increase of 2 percentage points from the month prior.

Fewer than 6 in 10 respondents to a Gallup poll released Tuesday said they expect to have a high quality of life over the next five years.

Developing

data.title: Want 'affordability?' Start by retooling your state's regressive tax system.
data.description:
The White House’s top economic advisors recently advised states to consider repealing taxes on corporate and personal income and to make up for it by drastically raising their sales taxes.

This is the last thing states should do if they want to make life more affordable for most people.

If enacted by every state, the White House plan “would represent the single largest legislated transfer of wealth from the working class to the rich in the nation’s history,” according to the Institute on Taxation and Economic Policy. “Working-class families would face dramatic tax increases while the nation’s wealthiest families would see their state tax bills plummet.”

Relying largely on consumption taxes and flat income tax rates, most state and local tax systems are already highly regressive — which means they garner a greater share of income from low- and middle-income families than from the wealthy. Over the last 30 years, many states  have lowered or compressed their rates, making them more regressive. Bucking this trend, a few recently have raised their top income tax rates into double digits. 

The White House is pushing states to mirror its regressive national tax policy of tax cuts primarily directed at the wealthiest alongside broad-based tariffs. Tariffs function a regressive national sales tax that dampens spending at the bottom of the economic pyramid the most.  

Democrats trying to raise or restructure state taxes face strong head winds.  In Virginia, after newly elected Gov. Abigail Spanberger (D) named affordability for Virginians as her top priority, Democrats in the state legislature proposed a raft of new taxes that have drawn considerable criticism. Advocates for low-income residents, along with many conservatives, objected to proposed higher sales taxes. Business lobbies oppose progressive proposals that would aggressively expand the top rungs of the state’s almost-flat income tax.

Spanberger inherited a budget surplus, but growth of Virginia’s economy has slowed. Lawmakers face funding gaps left by federal cuts in Medicaid, health insurance exchange subsidies, and other programs.

The commonwealth now relies heavily on regressive income, sales, and excise taxes. In contrast to the still-progressive federal income tax, Virginia’s almost-flat 5.75 percent income tax begins after the first $17,000 of income, such that “millionaires are in the same tax bracket as millions of Virginians with low and moderate incomes,” as the Commonwealth Institute puts it.

Virginia’s Gini co-efficient, a widely used measure of income inequality, is 46.9 — higher than most other states, and the U.S. is one of the most economically unequal countries in the world.

Democratic legislators, whatever their intentions, introduced a bill that would make the state’s tax system even more regressive and less affordable. The measure would let municipal governments pile extra local sales and use taxes on top of existing tax burdens.

Virginia’s general sales tax rate is 4.3 percent, plus a mandatory 1 percent local add-on tax across all localities. The combined general sales tax rate of 5.3 percent applies to most purchases. Northern Virginia and Hampton Roads have an extra 0.7 percent regional tax, bringing their total sales tax rate to 6 percent.

Virginia reduces its sales tax to 1 percent on most non-prepared food purchased for home consumption, such as groceries, and on essential personal hygiene products. Prescription and non-prescription medicines are generally exempt from sales tax. Under federal law, Virginia cannot apply sales tax to purchases made with SNAP benefits.

Virginia and other states could make life more affordable at the bottom by reducing their general sales tax rates, eliminating grocery taxes altogether, and making income taxes more progressive.  

Virginia could also increase revenue collected from corporations, which now contribute relatively little to state coffers. But tax changes should be moderate and gradual, and crafted to minimize impacts on economic growth.

It will take some time for Virginia and other states to find effective ways to make life more affordable.  Reducing the tax burden on low- and middle-income families should be part of their agenda.

In contrast, the tax policy advice from the White House’s economic brain trust would make life less affordable for most people. States should reject it.  

Karl Polzer is founder of the Center on Capital and Social Equity.
